description: The easiest edible to grow on a balcony. Salad leaves grow fast, tolerate partial shade, and can be harvested in as little as 3–4 weeks. Sow small amounts every 2 weeks for a continuous supply all summer.

characteristics.sunRequirement: Partial shade to full sun (3–6 hours)

pinchOutTops.info: Not typically required for this plant.

care.sowIndoors: Scatter seeds thinly on moist compost, barely cover them — they need light. Keep on a cool windowsill (10–15°C). Sprouts in 4–7 days.
care.sowOutdoors: From late March, sow directly into containers. Scatter seeds thinly, cover with a very thin layer of compost. Keep moist.
care.plantOut: Can go straight outside from late March. Lettuce tolerates light frost and actually prefers cooler weather.
care.watering: Keep consistently moist — dry soil causes bitter leaves and bolting. Water in the morning.
care.feeding: Light feeder — a handful of slow-release fertiliser in the compost at planting is enough.
care.harvest: Pick outer leaves as needed ('cut and come again'), or harvest whole heads. Pick before the plant bolts (sends up a flower stalk).
care.overwintering: Grow winter varieties (lamb's lettuce, winter gem) under fleece from September.

propagation.seedDescription: Very tiny, elongated seeds — either black or white depending on variety. Incredibly easy to grow.
propagation.germinationDays: 4–7 days at 10–20°C (needs light — don't bury deep)

deadheading.notes: Not applicable — harvest before flowering. If the plant bolts (sends up a tall flower stalk), the leaves turn bitter. Pull it out and sow fresh.

seedSaving.method: Let one plant bolt and flower. Wait for fluffy seed heads to form (like tiny dandelion clocks). Cut the whole stem and shake seeds into a bag.
seedSaving.seedPodDescription: Seeds form on dandelion-like fluffy heads at the top of the flower stalk.

troubleshooting: Bolting — shoots up and flowers in hot weather. Sow in partial shade and keep watered in summer.
Slugs and snails — the number one enemy. Use copper tape, beer traps, or raise pots off the ground.
Aphids — wash off with a jet of water or spray with diluted washing-up liquid.
funFact: Ancient Egyptians first cultivated lettuce over 4,500 years ago — but they grew it for the oil in its seeds, not for salad!
